chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Translator's Note

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: You have cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Sadly, about 40 percent of us will hear those three words within our lifetime,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and half will not survive.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: This means that two out of five of your closest friends and relatives

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: will be diagnosed with some form of cancer, and one will die.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Beyond the physical hardships,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: roughly one-third of cancer survivors here in the US

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: will go into debt from treatment,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and they're at least two and a half times more likely to declare bankruptcy

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: than those without cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: This disease is pervasive.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: It's emotionally draining, and for many, financially destructive.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: But a cancer diagnosis doesn't have to be a death sentence.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Finding cancer early, closer to its genesis,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: is one of the critical factors to improving treatment options,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: reducing its emotional impact, and minimizing financial burdens.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Most importantly, finding cancer early,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which is one of the primary aims of my research,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: greatly enhances your odds of survival.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: If we just look at the case of breast cancer, for example,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: we find that those who are diagnosed and treated at stage one

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: have a five-year survival rate of nearly 100 percent,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: odds that decrease to just 22 percent if treated at stage four.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: And similar trends are found for colorectal and ovarian cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Now, we're all aware that an early diagnosis that is accurate

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: is critical for survival.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: The problem is that many cancer diagnostic tools are invasive, costly,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: often inaccurate, and they can take an agonizing amount of time

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: to get the results back.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Still worse, when it comes to some forms of cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: such as ovarian, liver, or pancreatic cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: good screening methods simply don't exist,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: meaning that often people wait until physical symptoms surface,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which are themselves already indicators of late-stage progression.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Like a tornado strike in an area without an early warning system,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: there is no alarm to warn before the danger is already at your doorstep

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: when your odds of survival are greatly reduced.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Having the convenience and accessibility of regular screening options

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: that are affordable, non-invasive, and could provide results much sooner

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: would provide us with a formidable weapon in the fight against cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: An early warning would allow us to get out ahead of the disease

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: instead of merely following in its relentless wake.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: And this is exactly what I've been doing.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: For the past three years, I've been developing technologies

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: that could ultimately aid critical cancer testing

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and could ultimately aid clinicians with rapid early-stage cancer diagnostics.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: And I've been fueled by a deep scientific curiosity

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and a passion to change these statistics.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Last year, however, this fight became much more personal

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: when my wife was diagnosed with breast cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: It was an experience that added a strong and unexpected emotional dimension

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: to these efforts.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: I know firsthand how life-altering treatment can be,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and I'm keenly aware of the emotional havoc that cancer can wreak on a family,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which in our case included our two young daughters.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Because we found it early during a routine mammogram,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: we were able to focus primarily on treatment options for the localized tumor,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: reaffirming to me how important an early diagnosis is.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Unlike other forms of cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: mammograms do offer an early-stage screening option for breast cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Still, not everyone has this done,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: or they may develop breast cancer before the middle-age recommendation

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: for having a mammogram.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: So there's still a lot of room for improvement,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: even for cancers that do have screening options,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and of course, considerable benefits for those that don't.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: A key challenge then for cancer researchers

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: is to develop methods that make regular screening

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: for many types of cancers much more accessible.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Imagine a scenario where during your regular checkup,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: your doctor can take a simple non-invasive urine sample or other liquid biopsy

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and present you with the results before you even leave the doctor's office.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Such a technology could dramatically reduce the number of people

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: who slip through the net of an early-stage cancer diagnosis.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Now, my research team of engineers and biochemists

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: is working on exactly this challenge.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: We're working on ways to frequently activate an early-stage cancer alarm

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: by enabling regular screenings that would start when a person is healthy,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: so that action could be taken to stop cancer the moment it emerges

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and before it can progress beyond its infancy.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: The silver bullet in this case are tiny vesicles,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: little escape pods regularly shed by cells called exosomes.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Exosomes are important biomarkers that provide an early warning system

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: for the development of cancer.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: And because they are abundantly present in just about every bodily fluid,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: including blood, urine, and saliva,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: they are extremely attractive for non-invasive liquid biopsies.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: There's just one problem.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: An automated system for rapidly sorting these important parts of the body

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: before rapidly sorting these important biomarkers is not currently available.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: We've created a technology that we call NanoDLD that is capable of precisely this.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Automated exosome isolation to aid rapid cancer diagnostics.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Exosomes are the newest early warning weapon, if you will,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: to emerge on the liquid biopsy front, and they're really, really small.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: They measure just 30 to 150 nanometers in diameter.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: This is so tiny that you could fit about a million of them into a single red blood cell.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: That's roughly the difference between a golf ball and a fine-grained piece of sand.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Once thought to be little bins for unwanted cellular waste,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: it has been found that cells actually communicate by producing and absorbing these exosomes,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which contain surface receptors, proteins, and other genetic material collected from their cell of origin.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Now, when absorbed by a neighboring cell, exosomes release their contents into the receiving cell

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and can set in motion fundamental changes in gene expression.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Some good, and this is where cancer comes in.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Some bad.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Because they are clothed in the material of the mother cell and contain a sample of its environment,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: they provide a genetic snapshot of that cell's health and its origin.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: All of these qualities make exosomes invaluable messengers

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: that potentially allow physicians to eavesdrop on your health at the cellular level.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: To catch cancer early, however, you have to frequently intercept these messages

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: to determine when cancer-causing troublemakers within your body decide to start staging a coup,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which is why regular screening is so critical,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and why we're developing technologies to make this possible.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Now, while the first exosome-based diagnostics emerged on the market just this year,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: they are not yet part of mainstream healthcare options.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: In addition to their recent emergence, another factor that's limiting their widespread adoption

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: is that currently no automated exosome isolation system exists to make regular screening economically accessible.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: The current gold standard for exosome isolation includes ultracentrifugation,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: a process requiring expensive laboratory equipment, a trained lab tech, and about 30 hours of time to process a sample.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: We've come up with a different approach for achieving automated exosome isolation from a sample such as urine.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: We use a chip-based continuous flow separation technique called deterministic lateral displacement,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and we have done with it what the semiconductor industry has done so successfully for the past 50 years.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: We shrunk the dimensions of this technology from the micron scale to the true nanoscale.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: So how does it work?

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: In a nutshell, a set of tiny pillars separated by nanoscopic gaps are arranged in such a way

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: that the system divides the fluid into streamlines,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: with the larger cancer-related nanoparticles being separated through a process of redirection from the smaller, healthier ones,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which can in contrast move around the pillars in a zigzag-type motion in the direction of fluid flow.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: The net result is a complete separation of these two particle populations.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: You can visualize this separation process, similar to traffic on a highway, that separates into two roads,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: with one road going into a low-clearance tunnel under a mountain and the other road going around it.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Here, smaller cars can go through the tunnel, while larger trucks carrying potentially hazardous material are forced to take the detour route.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Traffic is effectively separated by size and contents without impeding its flow,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: and this is exactly how our system works on a much, much smaller scale.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: The idea here is that the separation process for screening could be as simple as processing a sample of urine, blood, or saliva,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: which is a near-term possibility within the next few years.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Ultimately, it could be used to isolate and detect target exosomes associated with a particular type of cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: sensing and reporting their presence within minutes.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: This would make rapid diagnostics virtually painless.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Broadly speaking, the ability to separate and enrich biomarkers with nanoscale precision in an automated way

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: opens the door to better understanding diseases such as cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: with applications ranging from sample preparation to diagnostics and from drug-resistance monitoring to therapeutics.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Even before my wife's bout with cancer, it was a dream of mine to facilitate the automation of this process,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: to make regular screening more accessible,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: similar to the way Henry Ford made the automobile accessible to the general population through development of the assembly line.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Automation is the key to accessibility.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: And in the spirit of the Hoover dream, a chicken in every pot and a car in every garage,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: we're developing a technology that could ultimately place an early warning cancer detection system in every home.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: This would allow every man, woman, and child the opportunity to be regularly tested while they're still healthy

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: catching cancer when it first emerges.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: It is my hope and dream to help people around the world avoid the high costs, physical, financial, and emotional

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: faced by today's cancer patients, hardships that I'm well acquainted with.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: I'm also happy to report that because we caught my wife's cancer early,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: her treatment was successful and she is now thankfully cancer-free.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: It is an outcome that I would like to see for everyone with a cancer diagnosis.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: With the work that my team has already done on separation of nanoscale biomarkers for rapid early stage cancer diagnostics,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: I am optimistic that within the next decade, this type of technology will be available,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: helping protect our friends, our family, and future generations.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Even if they are so unlucky as to be diagnosed with cancer,

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: that early stage alarm will provide a strong beacon of hope.

chunks.title: New_nanotech_to_detect_cancer_early
chunks.text: Thank you.

text: Translator's Note You have cancer. Sadly, about 40 percent of us will hear those three words within our lifetime, and half will not survive. This means that two out of five of your closest friends and relatives will be diagnosed with some form of cancer, and one will die. Beyond the physical hardships, roughly one-third of cancer survivors here in the US will go into debt from treatment, and they're at least two and a half times more likely to declare bankruptcy than those without cancer. This disease is pervasive. It's emotionally draining, and for many, financially destructive. But a cancer diagnosis doesn't have to be a death sentence. Finding cancer early, closer to its genesis, is one of the critical factors to improving treatment options, reducing its emotional impact, and minimizing financial burdens. Most importantly, finding cancer early, which is one of the primary aims of my research, greatly enhances your odds of survival. If we just look at the case of breast cancer, for example, we find that those who are diagnosed and treated at stage one have a five-year survival rate of nearly 100 percent, odds that decrease to just 22 percent if treated at stage four. And similar trends are found for colorectal and ovarian cancer. Now, we're all aware that an early diagnosis that is accurate is critical for survival. The problem is that many cancer diagnostic tools are invasive, costly, often inaccurate, and they can take an agonizing amount of time to get the results back. Still worse, when it comes to some forms of cancer, such as ovarian, liver, or pancreatic cancer, good screening methods simply don't exist, meaning that often people wait until physical symptoms surface, which are themselves already indicators of late-stage progression. Like a tornado strike in an area without an early warning system, there is no alarm to warn before the danger is already at your doorstep when your odds of survival are greatly reduced. Having the convenience and accessibility of regular screening options that are affordable, non-invasive, and could provide results much sooner would provide us with a formidable weapon in the fight against cancer. An early warning would allow us to get out ahead of the disease instead of merely following in its relentless wake. And this is exactly what I've been doing. For the past three years, I've been developing technologies that could ultimately aid critical cancer testing and could ultimately aid clinicians with rapid early-stage cancer diagnostics. And I've been fueled by a deep scientific curiosity and a passion to change these statistics. Last year, however, this fight became much more personal when my wife was diagnosed with breast cancer. It was an experience that added a strong and unexpected emotional dimension to these efforts. I know firsthand how life-altering treatment can be, and I'm keenly aware of the emotional havoc that cancer can wreak on a family, which in our case included our two young daughters. Because we found it early during a routine mammogram, we were able to focus primarily on treatment options for the localized tumor, reaffirming to me how important an early diagnosis is. Unlike other forms of cancer, mammograms do offer an early-stage screening option for breast cancer. Still, not everyone has this done, or they may develop breast cancer before the middle-age recommendation for having a mammogram. So there's still a lot of room for improvement, even for cancers that do have screening options, and of course, considerable benefits for those that don't. A key challenge then for cancer researchers is to develop methods that make regular screening for many types of cancers much more accessible. Imagine a scenario where during your regular checkup, your doctor can take a simple non-invasive urine sample or other liquid biopsy and present you with the results before you even leave the doctor's office. Such a technology could dramatically reduce the number of people who slip through the net of an early-stage cancer diagnosis. Now, my research team of engineers and biochemists is working on exactly this challenge. We're working on ways to frequently activate an early-stage cancer alarm by enabling regular screenings that would start when a person is healthy, so that action could be taken to stop cancer the moment it emerges and before it can progress beyond its infancy. The silver bullet in this case are tiny vesicles, little escape pods regularly shed by cells called exosomes. Exosomes are important biomarkers that provide an early warning system for the development of cancer. And because they are abundantly present in just about every bodily fluid, including blood, urine, and saliva, they are extremely attractive for non-invasive liquid biopsies. There's just one problem. An automated system for rapidly sorting these important parts of the body before rapidly sorting these important biomarkers is not currently available. We've created a technology that we call NanoDLD that is capable of precisely this. Automated exosome isolation to aid rapid cancer diagnostics. Exosomes are the newest early warning weapon, if you will, to emerge on the liquid biopsy front, and they're really, really small. They measure just 30 to 150 nanometers in diameter. This is so tiny that you could fit about a million of them into a single red blood cell. That's roughly the difference between a golf ball and a fine-grained piece of sand. Once thought to be little bins for unwanted cellular waste, it has been found that cells actually communicate by producing and absorbing these exosomes, which contain surface receptors, proteins, and other genetic material collected from their cell of origin. Now, when absorbed by a neighboring cell, exosomes release their contents into the receiving cell and can set in motion fundamental changes in gene expression. Some good, and this is where cancer comes in. Some bad. Because they are clothed in the material of the mother cell and contain a sample of its environment, they provide a genetic snapshot of that cell's health and its origin. All of these qualities make exosomes invaluable messengers that potentially allow physicians to eavesdrop on your health at the cellular level. To catch cancer early, however, you have to frequently intercept these messages to determine when cancer-causing troublemakers within your body decide to start staging a coup, which is why regular screening is so critical, and why we're developing technologies to make this possible. Now, while the first exosome-based diagnostics emerged on the market just this year, they are not yet part of mainstream healthcare options. In addition to their recent emergence, another factor that's limiting their widespread adoption is that currently no automated exosome isolation system exists to make regular screening economically accessible. The current gold standard for exosome isolation includes ultracentrifugation, a process requiring expensive laboratory equipment, a trained lab tech, and about 30 hours of time to process a sample. We've come up with a different approach for achieving automated exosome isolation from a sample such as urine. We use a chip-based continuous flow separation technique called deterministic lateral displacement, and we have done with it what the semiconductor industry has done so successfully for the past 50 years. We shrunk the dimensions of this technology from the micron scale to the true nanoscale. So how does it work? In a nutshell, a set of tiny pillars separated by nanoscopic gaps are arranged in such a way that the system divides the fluid into streamlines, with the larger cancer-related nanoparticles being separated through a process of redirection from the smaller, healthier ones, which can in contrast move around the pillars in a zigzag-type motion in the direction of fluid flow. The net result is a complete separation of these two particle populations. You can visualize this separation process, similar to traffic on a highway, that separates into two roads, with one road going into a low-clearance tunnel under a mountain and the other road going around it. Here, smaller cars can go through the tunnel, while larger trucks carrying potentially hazardous material are forced to take the detour route. Traffic is effectively separated by size and contents without impeding its flow, and this is exactly how our system works on a much, much smaller scale. The idea here is that the separation process for screening could be as simple as processing a sample of urine, blood, or saliva, which is a near-term possibility within the next few years. Ultimately, it could be used to isolate and detect target exosomes associated with a particular type of cancer, sensing and reporting their presence within minutes. This would make rapid diagnostics virtually painless. Broadly speaking, the ability to separate and enrich biomarkers with nanoscale precision in an automated way opens the door to better understanding diseases such as cancer, with applications ranging from sample preparation to diagnostics and from drug-resistance monitoring to therapeutics. Even before my wife's bout with cancer, it was a dream of mine to facilitate the automation of this process, to make regular screening more accessible, similar to the way Henry Ford made the automobile accessible to the general population through development of the assembly line. Automation is the key to accessibility. And in the spirit of the Hoover dream, a chicken in every pot and a car in every garage, we're developing a technology that could ultimately place an early warning cancer detection system in every home. This would allow every man, woman, and child the opportunity to be regularly tested while they're still healthy catching cancer when it first emerges. It is my hope and dream to help people around the world avoid the high costs, physical, financial, and emotional faced by today's cancer patients, hardships that I'm well acquainted with. I'm also happy to report that because we caught my wife's cancer early, her treatment was successful and she is now thankfully cancer-free. It is an outcome that I would like to see for everyone with a cancer diagnosis. With the work that my team has already done on separation of nanoscale biomarkers for rapid early stage cancer diagnostics, I am optimistic that within the next decade, this type of technology will be available, helping protect our friends, our family, and future generations. Even if they are so unlucky as to be diagnosed with cancer, that early stage alarm will provide a strong beacon of hope. Thank you.
